questions.question: A patient stood in a radiation field but has no radioactive material on or in them. This is:
options: C. Irradiation (exposure) — the patient is not radioactive
questions.rationale: Irradiation is exposure to a radiation field, like an X-ray. Once out of the field, the patient carries no radioactive material and cannot contaminate others.

questions.question: Which three principles protect responders from radiation?
questions.rationale: Minimizing time, maximizing distance, and interposing shielding are the fundamental radiation-protection levers.

questions.question: A contaminated patient also has an obstructed airway and severe hemorrhage. What is the correct priority?
options: A. Full decontamination before any care
B. Treat the life-threatening injuries first, then decontaminate
C. Give potassium iodide before anything else
D. Refuse care until a radiation officer arrives
questions.rationale: Trauma kills faster than external contamination. With standard precautions, airway and hemorrhage control come first; decon follows stabilization.

questions.question: Does standard PPE protect a responder from penetrating gamma radiation?
options: B. No — PPE prevents contamination but distance and shielding reduce penetrating dose
questions.rationale: PPE stops particles from landing on skin/airway (contamination) but does not block gamma rays; time, distance, and shielding govern penetrating dose.

questions.question: A 'dirty bomb' (radiological dispersal device) causes its GREATEST immediate harm through:
options: A. Acute radiation syndrome in everyone nearby
B. The conventional explosive blast, with contamination as a secondary and psychological threat
questions.rationale: An RDD's immediate casualties come from the blast; the scattered radioactive material is usually a contamination and fear problem rather than an immediate lethal radiation dose.

questions.question: In acute radiation syndrome, an earlier onset of vomiting indicates:
options: A. A lower dose and better prognosis
B. A higher dose and worse prognosis
questions.rationale: Time-to-vomiting is a rapid dose surrogate: the sooner and more severe the prodromal vomiting, the higher the dose received.

questions.question: Which body system is affected first (at the lowest whole-body doses causing ARS)?
questions.rationale: The hematopoietic system is most radiosensitive: falling blood counts cause infection and bleeding. GI and CV/CNS syndromes appear at progressively higher doses.

questions.question: What does potassium iodide (KI) protect against?
options: C. Thyroid uptake of radioactive iodine only
questions.rationale: KI saturates the thyroid to block radioiodine uptake, reducing thyroid-cancer risk. It does nothing for other radionuclides or external radiation.

questions.question: A nurse fears 'catching radiation' from a patient who was irradiated (exposed) but is not contaminated. The correct education is:
options: A. The fear is justified — avoid the patient
B. An irradiated, non-contaminated patient is not radioactive and poses no risk, like someone who had an X-ray
C. The patient must be isolated for a week
D. Only lead aprons allow contact
questions.rationale: Exposure is not contamination. A person who leaves a radiation field carries no source and cannot irradiate others — a key point for calming staff and enabling care.

questions.question: The single most effective step to reduce external radiological contamination on a patient is:
questions.rationale: Clothing removal eliminates the large majority of external contamination, mirroring chemical decon; gentle washing addresses the rest.

questions.question: When washing radiological contamination from intact skin and wounds, you should:
options: A. Scrub abrasively to remove every particle
B. Wash gently with soap and water, working from wounds outward, avoiding abrasion
questions.rationale: Gentle washing prevents driving contamination into the skin or wounds; working from wounds outward protects against internal contamination.

questions.question: Doubling your distance from a point radiation source affects dose how?
options: B. Reduces it dramatically (roughly to one quarter — inverse-square)
questions.rationale: Dose from a point source follows the inverse-square law, so doubling distance cuts dose to about one-quarter — distance is a powerful, immediate protection.

questions.question: Internal contamination occurs when radioactive material is:
options: A. In a field around the patient
B. On the patient's clothing only
C. Taken into the body via inhalation, ingestion, or wounds
D. Detected by a monitor across the room
questions.rationale: Internal contamination means the material is inside the body and requires medical management, unlike external contamination (removable) or irradiation (no material present).

questions.question: For which event is potassium iodide MOST relevant?
options: B. A release involving radioactive iodine (e.g., a reactor accident)
D. Any radiation event regardless of isotope
questions.rationale: KI is useful specifically when radioiodine is released; it blocks thyroid uptake. It is irrelevant for isotopes that are not iodine or for external exposure.

questions.question: Why is psychological/behavioral care a major component of radiological response?
options: B. Radiation fear is powerful and often exceeds the physical risk, generating worried-well surges and panic
C. Because radiation causes immediate psychosis
questions.rationale: The dread associated with radiation frequently produces large worried-well surges and psychological casualties that can overwhelm systems even when physical doses are low — clear communication is essential.

questions.question: A responder is wearing full PPE near a strong sealed gamma source and feels fully protected. The flaw is:
options: B. PPE does not stop gamma radiation; the responder still needs to limit time, maximize distance, and use shielding
questions.rationale: Contamination PPE gives a false sense of safety against penetrating gamma rays. Only time, distance, and shielding reduce that dose.
